coredata.dc:description: The freshwater shortage problem is increasing day after day. Solar stills are suitable devices to overcome this problem for small families and remote areas. However, these devices suffer from their low productivity. Two different modified designs for spinning wick solar stills were tested here. One distiller contained a rotating wick belt with a path of “LC” character shape (LC-RWSS), and the other with a path of “L” shape (L-RWSS). Additionally, the wick type was studied for black cotton and jute wicks. Moreover, the influence of belt-rotation direction (counterclockwise and clockwise) on the performance of solar still has been investigated. The period of wick belt rotation was tested (ON = 5 min and different OFF times = zero, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min). Also, the impact of utilizing graphene quantum dots nanofluid on the performance of LC-RWSS was considered. In addition, the effects of external reflectors and exhaust fan were examined. Results indicated that the jute wick provided more distillate than the cotton cloth. Furthermore, the productivity of LC-RWSS with counterclockwise movement of wick belt was always larger than that with clockwise direction under all different OFF times. In addition, the best output yield of LC-RWSS (at 0.1 rpm with jute wick-counterclockwise) was achieved when operating the wick belt under 30 min OFF and 5 min ON times, the LC still had a daily distillate more than L still by 17%. The thermal efficiency of L still and LC still was 82% and 86%, under 30 min OFF period of belt. With reflectors, exhaust fan and nano, the best output yield of LC-RWSS was provided when operating the belt under 10 min OFF and 5 min ON times. The LC still had a total yield more than that of the L still by 28% with 87% thermal efficiency. Also, the cost of freshwater obtained is 0.016 for LC still with reflectors, fan and nanofluids.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The freshwater shortage problem is increasing day after day. Solar stills are suitable devices to overcome this problem for small families and remote areas. However, these devices suffer from their low productivity. Two different modified designs for spinning wick solar stills were tested here. One distiller contained a rotating wick belt with a path of “LC” character shape (LC-RWSS), and the other with a path of “L” shape (L-RWSS). Additionally, the wick type was studied for black cotton and jute wicks. Moreover, the influence of belt-rotation direction (counterclockwise and clockwise) on the performance of solar still has been investigated. The period of wick belt rotation was tested (ON = 5 min and different OFF times = zero, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min). Also, the impact of utilizing graphene quantum dots nanofluid on the performance of LC-RWSS was considered. In addition, the effects of external reflectors and exhaust fan were examined. Results indicated that the jute wick provided more distillate than the cotton cloth. Furthermore, the productivity of LC-RWSS with counterclockwise movement of wick belt was always larger than that with clockwise direction under all different OFF times. In addition, the best output yield of LC-RWSS (at 0.1 rpm with jute wick-counterclockwise) was achieved when operating the wick belt under 30 min OFF and 5 min ON times, the LC still had a daily distillate more than L still by 17%. The thermal efficiency of L still and LC still was 82% and 86%, under 30 min OFF period of belt. With reflectors, exhaust fan and nano, the best output yield of LC-RWSS was provided when operating the belt under 10 min OFF and 5 min ON times. The LC still had a total yield more than that of the L still by 28% with 87% thermal efficiency. Also, the cost of freshwater obtained is 0.016 for LC still with reflectors, fan and nanofluids.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite the necessity of water, many organisms on Earth suffer from a lack of it [1]. And, because human civilization is at its pinnacle, experts have collaborated to propose several answers to the problem of water scarcity on the planet in order to secure the continuance of life without interruption. Scientists have presented various types of technique that desalinate ocean and sea water into freshwater that can be utilized for washing and drinking for animals and humans, industry and agriculture … etc. One of these techniques utilized globally: stage flashing, reverse osmosis, electrodialysis, humidification-dehumidification, effect boiling, and solar stills [2–6]. The solar energy is the base of all renewable energy sources [7–15]. Solar stills (SSs) are helpful for simple family use and in areas where sun energy is available, but they suffer from a low amount of production [16–18].

Some modifications that have contributed to improving the efficiency and productivity of the solar still are represented in the use of nanomaterials [19–23], quantum dots nanofluids [24,25], condensers [26–31], heat exchanger [32,33], mirrors [34–38], solar heaters [39–41], volcanic rocks [42], solar ponds [43], sun-tracking systems [44], wick materials [45–51], rotating parts [52–54], coated surfaces [55,56], PCM [57–59], and desiccant [60].

In addition, the nanofluids (quantum dots) were utilized for the renewable energy-powered systems due to their improved properties [61–65]. In addition, nanocomposites are utilized in several applications of solar systems [66–70]. All these modifications can be summed up in a parameter of increasing the temperatures difference between glass and water and reducing the depth of water in SSs [71–73]. The stills production would be augmented with increasing the temperature difference and lowering the depth of still water [74,75]. In addition, various types of distillers were conducted to improve their performance. So, the solar distillers of plastic water purifier [76], trays [77,78], stepped [79,80], drum [81], spherical/hemispherical [82,83], tubular [84–87], double-effect [88,89], pyramid [90–92], corrugated [46,93], and multi-stage [94] were conducted. The wick-type SS is a distiller with burlap material spread over an evaporative surface to enhance the vaporization of still. Some of burlap materials used were metal pieces, stones, jute, pin-ﬁnned material, cotton, rubber, and sponges were studied [95].

As previously tested and reported, using the condenser improved the performance of SS [89]. Reddy et al. [96] has reported that the production of a solar still with wick was improved by around 15–25% via utilizing the condenser. Pal et al. [97] undertook extensive theoretical study to investigate the effectiveness of a multi-wick double slope SS. They obtained 3.83% and 35% exergy and energy efficiency. The effect of several wicks, including black cotton and jute wick, was investigated [98]. The results indicated that, when compared to the jute wick, the black cotton wick produced more. Matrawy et al. [99] examined the SS performance with a corrugated-wick basin liner. The funding indicated that the production of the SS was enhanced when utilizing wick, nanofluids and reflectors.

It was tested how the employment of two stacks of jute and black cotton wicks affected the solar stills production running in varied water depths [100]. The pile of jute cloth generated more than the pile of black cotton cloth. Arunkumar et al. [101] investigated the distiller output when the evaporative surface was coated with CuO nanoparticles and sponges were used. They showed that sponges were less effective than covering the absorber with CuO-Nano. Agrawal and Rana [102] used floating wicks in the shape of a V to speed up the evaporation process of the distillers. The distiller productivity increased by 56.62%. Gad et al. [103] demonstrated a horizontally spinning belt inside a basic slanted wick solar still.

The novelty of this research can be demonstrated by evaluating two different designs (LC and L design) of rotating wick solar stills. Both designs are based on the use of wick material in the vertical, horizontal, and inclined space within the solar still space without increasing the land's horizontal occupied area but by optimizing the utilize only the vertical space. The wick material was utilized as a rotary belt on the previously marked tracks (LC and L tracks). Additionally, the type of wick was studied for black cotton and jute wicks. Also, the impact of belt-rotation direction (CW and CCW direction) on the performance of RWSS still has been investigated. In addition, the belt was run for a defined period (5 min ON time) and then paused for various periods (60, 50, 40, 30, 20, 10, and zero minutes OFF times) to evaluate its effect on distillation performance. Furthermore, the effects of external reflectors and exhaust fan were examined. Finally, the effect of utilizing graphene quantum dots nanofluid of RWSS production was studied.

An experimental test rig was built, mounted, and constructed using SSs modified with the novel designs with the presence of a conventional SS to be measured with, with the presence of some tools and measuring devices that help the continuity of the operation of the tested solar stills such as a valves and tank of salt water … etc. The details are indicated in Figs. 1 and 2 . The tank of saline water was 1.0 m height × 0.5 m diameter. The modified rotating wick solar stills were nominated by LC-RWSS (for the distiller with revolving belt in the shape of LC) and L-RWSS (for the distiller with revolving belt in the shape of L). Fig. 2 shows a schematic view for the tested rotating wick solar stills.

The L-RWSS basin area was 0.5 m2. L-RWSS's main body was built of 1.5 mm thick galvanized steel. Also, the four SSs' walls were constructed from 4 mm thick glass. The back and front walls were 95 and 15 cm, Fig. 2. Also, the still cover was constructed from 4 mm thick glass. The solar still was contained within a wooden box with a 5 cm gap between them. This area was filled with sawdust to serve as an insult. The belt was still rotated by four rotating pulleys. The condensed droplets were collected in four troughs mounted on the still's inner glass walls.

LC still had the same land area as L still and was made of the same material. Furthermore, the back and front walls of the LC still measured 95 and 40 cm, respectively. The belt was rotated by 6 revolving pulleys on the LC still. The LC-RWSS had a spinning wick belt with a "L" track that was terminated by a chamfer. The goal of this design was to improve vaporization within the basin.

The basin of the tested RWSS was 10 cm high on all sides and exposed to sun radiation, transferring thermal energy to the base and from there to the water. Furthermore, the redesigned distiller's front section was directly exposed to sun radiation. As a result, the RWSS' bases were built of galvanized steel. The wick belt was constructed from the tension of the wick material as it has the width of the RWSS and the length of the belt. The length of belt was 3.64 and 3.4 m for LC still and L still. For both RWSSs, the wide of belt was 0.46 m. The rotating rollers' surfaces have been roughened to prevent the hessian from sliding over them without rotation. All components that require power to function have been linked to a 6-W DC motor with solar panels to eliminate the need for traditional energy.

The external bottom and top reflectors are of 1 m long and 0.5 m wide as shown in Fig. 2. Seasonally, the top mirror can be angled either backwards or forwards. The top mirror should be slightly angled forward during the winter. However, the top mirror should be slightly angled backwards in the summer. More information about reflector inclination angles might be obtained from other publications [36,37]. In addition, Fig. 2 has indicated the inclination angles for bottom reflectors (θB) and external top reflectors (θT). The tested solar distiller is always pointed south to receive the most sun rays. A fan with a 6 W motor and a diameter of 10 cm is fixed to the front wall of LC-RWSS. It is used to extract steam to an external condenser (feed water tank) and speed up the condensation process, as shown in Fig. 2.

The experimental testing time began at 8:00 a.m. and still through the daytime to 06:00 p.m. Additionally, the freshwater was measured hourly. The depth of saline water inside RWSSs was 1 cm. A graded glass tube with a letter L form was installed at the back side of the SS to show the height of water. The amount of feed water from the feed tank was controlled using a valve. To keep the water level at 1 cm, the feeding process was repeated every half hour. The belt's bottom side is in contact with the basin's water, and it revolves at 0.1 rpm. First, the impact of the different designs (LC-RWSS and L-RWSS) was tested. After that, the wick type was investigated for black jute and cotton wicks. Then, the impact of belt-rotation direction (CW and CCW directions) on the still performance has been investigate. Additionally, the belt was run for a fixed time (5 min ON time) and stopped for various time (60, 50, 40, 30, 20, 10 and zero minutes OFF times) to investigate its impact on the RWSS performance. Finally, the use of graphene quantum dots nanofluid of still yield was investigated.

The sun intensity was determined by a solarimeter (0–5000W/m2). Also, an anemometer (0.4–30 m/s) was used to measure the velocity of air. Additionally, thermocouples-k-type were utilized to know the temperatures at different points of setup. With the help of an Arduino plate, the thermocouple signals were converted into digital temperature measurements. Furthermore, calibrated graduated flasks (5 mL accuracy and capacity of 2 L) were used to quantize and collect the stills production of RWSSs.

Consequently, the resulting error of the calculated ƞ was about ±2.7%.

In Fig. 3 with OFF time = 30 min, the per-hour intensity and temperatures are displayed. Fig. 3 shows that LC distiller retained water temperatures that were between 0 and 4° Celsius warmer than those of the L still. This may have occurred because the wick used for wrapping the L receiver was bigger than that used to wrap the LC receiver. As a result, the wick materials in L-distiller received some radiated energy to a greater extent than LC-distiller. Additionally, a portion of water in LC-distiller is immediately subjected to the irradiation. As a result, the water in LC was heated to a higher temperature than in L. The LC-distiller's high evaporation caused its glass temperature to increase by 0–2 °C in comparison to L-distiller.

Additionally, Fig. 4 shows the variations in outputs for each device at OFF time = 30 min. The yield of distillers started out at zero in the early hours and reached its peak at midday. The maximum outputs of L-distiller and LC-distiller were discovered to be 1100 mL and 1300 mL, accordingly, at 1:00 p.m. Additionally, the LC-distiller and L-still total outputs were 9600 and 8200 mL/m2, respectively. As a result, the improvement rate rose to 17%. Following that, as irradiation decreased over time, the distillers' production started to fall (Fig. 3). As well, Fig. 4 establishes that the yield of LC-still was greater than that of L-still. Three primary causes are cited for this improvement. The primary factor is that the LC-still has a larger surface area available to absorb sun irradiation than the L-still. As shown in Fig. 2, the contact area of the L-still is approximately 0.81 m2, whereas that of the LC-still is 1.16 m2. Additionally, the evaporating surface of LC still was greater than that of L still, increasing the LC still's rate of vaporization. In this case, the L still has an area of 1.17 m2. Evaporation happens on both of the wick belt's surfaces as a result of the wicking being placed between the rolls. Thus, 2 × 1.17 = 2.34 m2 may be used to compute the entire surface of vaporization for L-still. In contrast, the evaporating surface of LC-still is 1.52 m2. Because the wicking was suspended on rolls, evaporation occurred from both perspectives of the wicking. The entire evaporation surface is thus equivalent to 2 (1.52–0.125) + 0.125, which is 2.9 m2. As a consequence of increased surface of vaporization, the LC-still speed of vaporizing is higher compared to that of L-still. Additionally, relative to L-still, LC-still exhibited greater condensate. In this case, the L still featured 4 glazed top, side, and rear condensate interfaces. Additionally, the vertical glass front of LC-still acts as a condensing area. Likewise, the temperature of glass's backwards was 0–8 °C fewer than the upper glazing. The left and right sides of LC-still exhibited temperatures that were 0–2.7 °C lower than those of the top glass. Where the vertical sides were shielded from the sun's rays.

The typical still has a mean yield of around 2500 mL/day, in accordance with [105]. As a consequence, when using LC-still and L-still, the yield of SS may be enhanced by 208% and 168%, respectively, when compared to those of Ref. [105]. The subsequent explanations are given for this improvement. The initial step is to encase the wicking surface with less salty water as feasible. As a consequence, a revolving still evaporates water more quickly than a standard still. The additional explanation is that the boundary layer is broken by rotating wicking. This enhanced vapor in the distiller. Additionally, pulling the wicking belts results in induced evaporation, which raises the level of vaporization. In addition, L-still and LC-still have higher wick evaporation areas and more exposed areas to irradiation than a traditional still.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LC-still with wick belt rotating in two directions and various OFF time
ce:section.ce:para: To create the ideal circumstances for the operation of the LC-still, the effects of the wicking belt's rotational orientation and OFF Time have been examined. Consequently, Fig. 5 illustrates the rise in distillation production of LC-still at continuous ON (5 min) and varied OFF periods with clockwise (CW) and counterclockwise (CCW) rotations of the belts. The graph shows that, at all OFF periods, spinning the jute belt of LC-still in the CCW direction produced a higher output than doing it in CW direction. This could be because, in CCW, the belt moves such that the side with the greatest saturation point meets the back glass with the smallest glazing temperature. The moist side of the wicking strip was also towards the rear glazing surface, shielding it from the sun's rays. Because of gravitation and evaporation, some water evaporates off of this surface. The action of evaporation occurred quickly since the belt surfaces are subjected straight to solar light and have small water content. Whereas for CCW movement, the wicking surface that is immediately subjected to solar irradiation was more saturated with liquid. The output increment of LC-still for CCW and CW movements, accordingly, was 17% and 15% under 30 min of belt OFF time. Additionally, the production of LC-still with CW movement was nearly identical to that with CCW movement when the OFF duration of the wicking belt was lengthened. It happens since the water was pouring into the basin as a result of the force of gravity, which minimizes the impact of the rotation's orientation. As a result, the rise in production of LC-still with CW and CCW movements during 60 min OFF time was 13.8% and 14.2%, correspondingly. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 5, at OFF time = 0 min, the overall output of LC still is higher than that of the L distiller. With CCW movements, the daily output increased by 15% from the L distiller's 6650 mL/m2 to LC distiller's 7650 mL/m2 output. Furthermore, the output of the LC-still increased by roughly 12.9% while using CW movement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LC-distiller with different wicks and CCW movement
ce:section.ce:para: The functionality of the LC-distiller has been investigated in relation to the use of cotton and jute wicking materials. As a result, Fig. 6 shows how the LC-distiller's increased output while using cotton and jute wicks and CCW movement. The daily rise in production of LC-still with jute fabric was larger than that with cotton cloth during all OFF durations for the first 60 min, as shown in Fig. 6. This may be due to the cotton wick's large water capacity as opposed to the jute wick's. This is as a result of the jute material having larger line offsets than cotton. Additionally, the production of LC-distiller with jute was comparable to that with cotton with an increase in OFF time of the wicks. It happened by the fact that the water moved into the basin as a result of gravitation, which minimizes the impact of the wicks. While employing cotton and jute clothing, the output of LC-still rose by 11% and 15% within OFF duration of 0 min (continuous rotating). However using jute and cotton fabrics during 30 min of off time increased the production of LC-still by 17% and 14%, respectively. Whenever jute and cotton cloths have been employed, the production of LC-still rose by 15.3% and 14.2%, accordingly, at 60 min off. This is due to the fact that high pauses induce dry spots in the jute belt, which significantly reduces its output, but the cotton belt stays completely wet since cotton transfers a sizable quantity of water at startup.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condenser's energy efficiency (back side of glass)
ce:section.ce:para: The LC-still exhibited lower temperatures for the vertically glazing walls (front, rear, and right sides) than the above glazing cover, according to the aforementioned studies. We concentrated on learning about the thermal evaluation of these surfaces as a result. As a consequence, Table 2 shows the variations in temperature of the ambient air and vertical surfaces with glass covers for LC-still at 0 min OFF time. The temperatures behaved similarly to the irradiation. Temperatures rise in direct proportion to sun irradiation, and inversely. Additionally, it was discovered that the glazing temperature was 0.5–7.5 °C more than the temperature of the vertical glazing rear surfaces. Due to the glass rear surface not facing the sun directly, this occurred. Because of this, the LC-distiller's vertical glazing rear area served as an interior dehumidifier. Likewise, the temperatures of the glass covering were between 0.5 and 2.5 °C greater than those of the right and left sides of the glass vertical walls. The temperature on the left glass side was also cooler throughout the early hours than on the opposite side. This is a result of the glass' right surface facing the sun immediately. Over the course of the day, this move was reversed. The direct sun irradiation beams on both surfaces caused the temperatures of the right and left sides to be identical at one point (about 1:00 p.m.).

Additionally, Table 2 highlights the variations in outputs offered by the LC-still's vertically glazing surfaces at 0 min OFF time. The LC-still's overall output is a combination of the outputs for the front, rear, right, and left surfaces of glazing. Table 3 s findings revealed that the top, rear, and both left and right surfaces contributed, in that order, by 33%, 29%, and 38% of the overall yield attained by LC-still. As a consequence of its lower temperature relative to all other surfaces, the rear glass surface produced the greatest amount of production. This is because the rear glazing has a far warmer glass-water temperature than the other faces. The output of the right side was also smaller during the early morning time than that of the glass left surface, and vice versa during the afternoon session. Due to the sun's energy landing straight on the right and left areas throughout the morning and afternoon hours, accordingly, this activity took place.

Fig. 6 shows further data concerning the impact of running the wicking belt at varied OFF times. It is important to note that the testing under various settings were carried out in comparable circumstances to that shown in Fig. 6. The daily distiller output grew when the OFF time was extended from 0 to 30 min, and then it decreased till 60 min. According to Fig. 6, for all of the examined OFF periods, the LC-still had a higher yield than the L-still. This resulted from the LC still's better condensing and vaporizing capabilities versus the L still.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LC-distiller with reflectors and exhaust fan
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of LC-still with and without external mirrors is study, Fig. 7 . The daily productivity rises of the LC- still with and without reflectors compared to the L-still is shown in Fig. 7. It can be concluded that using the combined system of top and bottom external reflectors introduced superior productivity compared to that without the reflectors. This is because of the increased and focused solar energy input to the LC- still with mirrors. One of the benefits of the lower mirror is directing the solar radiation on the lower inclined part, as it was not exposed to any solar radiation.

It can be noted that the effect of reflectors on productivity rise of LC-still is weak at OFF Time 50 and 60 min due to the appearance of dry areas on the belt as a result of the increase OFF Time and high energy exposed to the belt. At OFF Time of 10 min, the rate of increase is 5.6% compared to the absence of reflectors (21.5% with mirrors and 15.9% without mirrors). While, the effect of mirrors is higher at lower OFF Time starting from 0 min, the rate of increase in productivity reaches about 2%. But the rate of increase decreases at higher OFF Time due to the increase in stop time. At OFF Time of 30 min, the rate reaches 3% (20% with mirrors and 17% without mirrors). While, at OFF Times of 50 and 60 min, the rate reaches 0.8% and 0.5%.

The results indicated that LC-still glass temperature is high as a result of employing the external reflectors. The high temperature of glass cover and walls has a negative impact on the condensation process. Therefore, the created water vapor from the LC-still has been extracted and condensed externally using a fan and an external condenser. In order to enhance the process of condensation, an axial fan and an external condenser (feed water tank) are utilized to lower the temperature of the LC-still glass cover and glass walls. When reflectors and exhaust fan are employed, the LC-still glass temperatures show about 0–2.5 °C higher than those of the L-still. With reflectors, the high vaporization of LC-still led to raise its glass temperature compared to that of the L-still by about 0–5 °C (while it was 0–2 °C without reflectors). It should be noted that the temperatures of the basin water and glass are lower than they were in the absence of the external condenser. This occurs because the suction fan reduces the internal pressure of the LC-still, lowering the temperature of the saline water and, as a result, the temperature of the evaporated water vapor. Furthermore, the suction fan exhausts the majority of the water vapor contained within the LC-still to the condenser. Low-temperature water vapor will consequently condense in a limited amount on the internal surface of the glass cover. This results in a lower glass cover temperature than in the preceding scenario. At OFF Time of 10 min, the rate of increase is 3% compared to the absence of reflectors (21.5% without fan and 24.5% with fan).

This part looked into the effects of using quantum dots nanofluids. According to the experimental findings, employing quantum dots nanofluids enhances LC-still effectiveness. With 10 min of off time, the production rate of LC-still with mirrors and fan increased by 24.5% and 28% with and without nanofluids, respectively. This indicates that the yield increased by roughly 3.5% as a result of utilizing nanofluids.

The thermal effectiveness of distillers is a crucial factor in determining whether or not the distiller is practical and cost-effective. According to Ref. [86], the thermal efficiency is calculated:

Because of the DC motor power's low value of 6 W, the findings demonstrated that the efficiency was independent of it. However, it is acknowledged that the wicking belt's OFF periods had a major role in efficacy. The LC-still with reflectors, fans, and nanofluids was found to be more efficient than the L-still and without them. For instance, the L-still had an energy efficiency of 74% when the belt was completely off. Additionally, the LC-still thermal efficiency under conditions of 0% belt OFF time was 82.5% with all adjustments and 77% without adjustments. Additionally, L-still had a 77% efficiency after only 10 min of being off. Additionally, the efficacy of the LC-still under 10 min of belt OFF time was 87% with all adjustments and 82% without adjustments.

The water and system expenses both affect how effectively distilling systems function. The specifics of the fixed expenses for the two evaluated distillers are shown in Table 4 . The specifics of how the economic analyses was calculated may be found in Ref. [73]. According to the formulae in Table 5 , the cost of freshwater for L-still is 0.027 $/L. Additionally, the cost of obtaining freshwater for the LC-still after all changes is 0.016 $/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of this study's findings with earlier findings
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the advantages of this research over additional investigations, further comparisons between the findings of this research and those from different investigations are provided as well. In Table 6 , the full comparison is shown. It was determined that the suggested changes will significantly increase functionality. The suggested changes made use of a revolving wicking belt with two separate L and LC route configurations. Additionally, using nanofluids made of graphene quantum dots has helped the LC-still work better.

Two designs of rotating wick SSs (L-RWSS design and LC-RWSS design) were design and tested. Moreover, the impact of direction of belt-rotation (CW and CCW directions) on the performance of solar still has been investigated. Furthermore, the belt was run for a fixed period (5 min ON time) and stopped for different periods (zero, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min OFF times) to investigate its influence on the RWSS performance. In addition, the effects of external reflectors and exhaust fan were examined. Also, the effect of utilizing graphene quantum dots nanofluid of still production was considered. The results indicated that the design of LC-RWSS achieved better performance than either the conventional rotating wick distiller (L-RWSS). The results back up the following.
•
The LC-RWSS productivity with CCW direction movement of wick belt was larger than that with CW direction under all various OFF times. the productivity rise of LC-RWSS was 15% and 17%for CW and CCW motions, under 30 min OFF time of belt.
•
The jute wick provided more distillate than the cotton cloth. Where, the production of LC-RWSS was raised by 14% and 17% when utilizing cotton and jute wicks, at 30 min OFF Time.
•
At 0.1 rpm with jute wick-counterclockwise, the best daily production LC still was provided when operating the belt under 30 min OFF and 5 min ON times, the LC still had a daily production more than that of the L still by around 17%. The thermal efficiency of L still and LC still was 82% and 86%, respectively, under 30 min OFF period of belt.
•
With reflectors, exhaust fan and nano (At 0.1 rpm with jute wick-counterclockwise), the best daily production of LC still was provided when operating the belt under 10 min OFF and 5 min ON times. The LC still had a daily production more than that of the L still by 28% with 87% thermal efficiency.
•
The freshwater cost obtained is 0.016 for LC-RWSS with reflectors, fan and nanofluids.
•
When compared to previous research, it was obtained that the studied SS unit is and efficient and productive, that it may be applied in desert and remote areas.
